problem.title:
Your NPCs are puppets.
Players can tell.
problem.lede: Thirty years of game AI — state machines and behaviour trees — encode the designer's intentions, never the agent's. The NPC has no standpoint, no memory of you, and cannot surprise its own creator.

stats.head: of developers are excited about AI NPCs
stats.body: and roughly half expect 40%+ of studios to be building with them within five years.

stats.head: of gamers say advanced AI NPCs would improve gameplay
stats.body: near-universal appetite for characters that remember you and react like they mean it.

stats.head: generative AI in gaming by 2033
stats.body: from ~$1.1B in 2023; the believable-NPC frontier is where that spend is heading.

problem.clock: The labs have shipped the pieces — Generative Agents, Voyager, SIMA, Project Sid. What's missing is one architecture that is believable, lifelong, social, AND cheap enough to run for a thousand NPCs in real time. That is the gap.

product.title:
Not a smarter script.
A cognitive cycle.
product.lede: Each tick, the world hands the mind a percept; the mind hands back one bounded action and one legible thought. In between runs a fixed seven-step cycle — the BDI loop wearing a dual-process coat.

flow.lab: one cognitive cycle per tick — fast almost always, slow only when it matters

features.p: Beliefs that decay out of sight — so the agent can be wrong, mistaken, and surprised.
Homeostatic needs plus intrinsic curiosity. It wants things; nobody scripts 'go eat'.
A predator at arm's reach triggers flight with no deliberation at all.
Cheap System-1 arbitration — or escalate to System-2 when surprised, risky, or torn.
Episodic with salience recall and forgetting, semantic facts, a growing skill library.
An adopted goal persists (Bratman) — so a mind with two needs doesn't dither and starve.
Models the other agents it meets; relationships carry history (ToMnet).
Periodically distils raw experience into durable facts, skills, and self-concept.
Every decision narrated and tagged REFLEX / fast / SLOW — the mind made visible.

yaml: // System 2 plugs in here — one trait.
// Each tick: one action, one thought.

benchmarks.title: One engine. Five minds.
benchmarks.lede: Five personas differ only in three trait scalars — boldness, sociability, curiosity — layered over identical code. Run in the same world for 400 ticks, they live measurably different lives. None of it is scripted.
benchmarks.method: Daimon ships a deterministic, offline build: no model weights, no network. An honest HeuristicDeliberator stands in for the language model, so what you observe is the architecture itself, not a model's eloquence. Every number below is produced by the included demo at seed 0xDA13 and reproducible with one command.

overhead.title: Conversations over a 400-tick life
overhead.sub: Same code, same map — sociability alone reshapes the life.
overhead.note: The social agent crosses the map again and again to be near others — and takes more predator hits doing it. The curious one ignores company to study a monolith. Emergent, not authored.

percentiles.title: Deliberation stays ~10%
percentiles.note: ~90% of cognition is near-free System-1. The model is spent only on the moments that matter.

optimisation.title: Health at 400 ticks (%)
optimisation.note: Bold runs hot and near the edge; social courts risk for company. The architecture, not a difficulty slider.

bignums.p: tests passing, clippy-clean — emergent behaviour kept under regression test.
lines of Rust across four crates; a whole mind is a serialisable value.
network calls in the demo: fully deterministic and offline, same seed → same life.

scaling.title: Why a thousand NPCs is affordable.
scaling.sub: The escalation policy turns 'one model call per NPC per frame' (impossible) into 'one per interesting moment' (a fleet).
scaling.note: Everything System 1 handles — locomotion, routine foraging, reflexive flight, holding a commitment — costs nothing but local compute. The expensive reasoner is spent only where it changes the outcome: novel situations, hard trade-offs, social nuance, recovering from surprise. In our runs that is ~10% of ticks, debounced by a cooldown — a handful of model calls per agent per minute instead of thousands.

security.title: Honest status — concept, not runtime.
security.lede: The architecture is complete and tested. What remains is mostly integration — a model, an engine — not unbuilt cognition. This is the line between what runs today and what is designed-for.
rows: Perceive → appraise → reflex → decide → plan → act → reflect, built and tested
Episodic (salience recall + forgetting), semantic, procedural, spatial map
Per-agent models with disposition and relationship history
One engine yielding five distinct, emergent lives
Seeded PRNG; reproducible lives; 13 passing tests
Trait defined + documented; wire to Claude (ReAct / Reflexion / ToT)
Principled surprise and planning in unknown dynamics (MuZero-style)
Bevy / Unity / Unreal via the bounded action surface
SIMA-style sensing instead of a structured percept
A Project Sid / PIANO-style cognitive society
security.note: SHIPPED means built and tested in the concept repository; ROADMAP means designed-for, with the seam already in place. Full detail in ROADMAP.md and WHITEPAPER.md §7.

voices.title: What it stands on.

quotes.q: A believable agent is a memory stream, plus reflection, plus planning over it.
Lifelong competence comes from a growing library of skills and a self-set curriculum.
Think fast by default; think slow only when it pays — and budget the slow path.
Curiosity is the reward for the kind of surprise that improves your model of the world.
An intention, once adopted, must persist — or a mind with two wants achieves neither.

voices.disclaimer: These are descriptions of published ideas that Daimon composes, attributed to their sources — not endorsements of this work. ReAct, Reflexion, Tree-of-Thoughts, ToMnet, GOAP and HTN round out the lineage; full citations are in WHITEPAPER.md.

tooling.title: Four crates, one mind.
tooling.lede: A Cargo workspace. The cognition is mechanism-free types in core; the thinking lives in mind; the world is a small testbed; the demo lets you watch a life unfold.

cards.p: The cognitive type system. With only serde as a dependency, a whole mind is a plain serialisable value you can snapshot, diff, and replay.
The seven-step cycle, commitment with hysteresis, the planner, theory of mind, and the Deliberator seam where a language model plugs in.
A tiny deterministic grid world — just enough stakes that any lifelike behaviour you see comes from the mind, not from elaborate scenery.

cards.code:
$ cargo run --bin daimon
    --persona social
    --ticks 400

# a narrated life +
# end-of-life review
cards.p: Drops a mind into the world and prints its narrated inner life, then a life-in-review: beliefs, skills, relationships, and most-vivid memories.

engage.title: From concept to a cast of thousands.
engage.lede: The architecture is done and the seams are in place. Three moves turn the concept into a runtime.

tiers.name: Give it a voice
items: Implement the LlmDeliberator behind the existing trait
Render context → prompt; parse a goal + chain-of-thought
Same ~10% cost envelope — a few model calls per agent per minute

tiers.name: Give it a body
items: Embed in Bevy / Unity / Unreal via the bounded action surface
Continuous space and navmesh in place of grid steps
Persist a live mind to disk; resume; author its memories

tiers.name: Give it a society
items: Many fully-cognitive agents sharing one world
Emergent roles, economy, and culture (Project Sid / PIANO)
A director layer that sets soft goals for a whole cast
